text:
The advisory systems that form part of a country’s policy advisory system are under intense pressure in crisis times to come up with high quality advice that is based on empirical analysis. This pressure inevitably generates conflicts about what needs to be done to deal with the crisis and tensions are heightened by the inevitable lack of knowledge in key areas and by the unpredictability of the unfolding crisis. Governments have to deal with numerous pressures, constraints and uncertainties whether it be a financial, political, health or security crisis. Recent experience with the COVID-19 crisis has highlighted this pressure and the potential for very different advisory responses across different countries.

This introductory article aims at putting together a number of papers that look at the policy advisory systems and offer new insights into the ways these systems dealt with the challenges of COVID-19. Almost all policy advice was formulated in an atmosphere of great public controversy and politics surrounding the management of the health vs economic vs social crisis. In this Special Issue, we examine policy advisory systems through three prisms: (i) the availability, quality of data and information that could be used for policy-making; (ii) the composition of advisory councils including the representation of different types of experts and the balance between different types of knowledge; and (iii) the ways in which experts performed their roles – whether they were formalists or critical publicists, civil servants or academics or representatives of other types of civil society organizations and networks. We also discuss the distinctions between experts as purveyors of knowledge or as knowledge shapers as well as the range of ceremonial and informal activities they engage in with politicians. We extend the research focus to creeping crises – such as climate change – and offer new insights into the ways in which expertise and knowledge are used for crisis governance and the dynamics of the relationship between policy advisors and politicians in conditions of crisis.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
